ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry rice dish class; no specific Japanese vegetable curry rice entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with vegetables and curry sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Japanese vegetable curry rice code. A0CKW (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for Japanese vegetable curry rice; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Japanese vegetable curry rice is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice topped with 150 g of vegetable curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetables Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010939 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese vegetable curry rice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the analogous entry for white rice with mixed vegetable curry (GI ~50–58 range) from Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed GI tables. The high-fibre vegetable content (3.7 g/100g) and curry sauce matrix moderately attenuate the GI of the white rice base (GI ~72 plain). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (54) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) = 54 × 88.2 g / 100 = 47.6; however, using a standard 200 g rice + 150 g curry split and blended carbohydrate of ~69.7 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving: GL = 54 × 69.7 / 100 = 37.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing ~35% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium curry roux and limit portion size to one plate, as cumulative sodium from a full serving approaches one-third of the recommended daily maximum; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets the sodium load in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base is high GI (~72 plain); blended dish GI estimated at 54 (low) due to vegetable fibre 3.7 g/100 g moderating glycaemic response; carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should practise strict portion control (ideally ≤250 g per meal), substitute brown or basmati rice for the white rice base to lower the glycaemic index, and request less potato in the curry to reduce glycaemic load; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre content is beneficial for post-prandial glucose moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21 g/100 g is low; cholesterol confirmed 0 mg/100 g; iron 2.55 mg/100 g and potassium 327 mg/100 g support cardiovascular health; sodium at 231 mg/100 g warrants monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetables curry rice is relatively low in total fat and contains no cholesterol, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; however, sodium intake should be monitored across the full day, and the curry roux should be checked for saturated fat content from palm oil or dairy, which could elevate LDL cholesterol if consumed frequently.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curry spices (turmeric, ginger) provide curcumin and gingerol with anti-inflammatory properties; nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory compounds in turmeric and ginger present in the curry spice blend, but should monitor joint symptoms if the dish contains nightshade vegetables such as potato, eggplant, or tomato, as solanine may aggravate inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetables Curry Rice (野菜カレーライス, Yasai Karē Raisu) is a Japanese-style dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside or topped with a thick, mildly spiced vegetable curry sauce. The curry sauce is typically prepared using a Japanese curry roux block (which contains wheat flour, curry spices, and fat), simmered with vegetables such as potato, carrot, onion, and other seasonal vegetables. The dish is a staple of Japanese home cooking and is widely served in school canteens, cafeterias, and casual restaurants across Japan and Singapore. The flavour profile is mild, slightly sweet, and savoury, with a thick, glossy sauce that coats the rice. The texture contrasts soft, starchy rice with tender vegetable pieces. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The relatively high fibre content from mixed vegetables moderates the glycaemic impact of the white rice base. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan (curry roux may contain dairy) and is not gluten-free due to wheat in the roux. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (231 mg/100 g) requires attention for hypertensive individuals, while the low estimated GI of 54 and fibre content make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes with portion control. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger) may benefit individuals with inflammatory conditions. This dish is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner in Singapore and Japan.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00010939, confidence 0.95, provenance canonical, review_status reviewed). The source is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from an analogous peer-reviewed source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct measurement exists, which reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong nutrient provenance.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Japanese vegetable curry rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010939) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): the HPB source explicitly reports 0.0 mg cholesterol per 100 g for this dish, consistent with a vegetable-only curry containing no animal-derived ingredients in the main components. 3. GI value of 54 is an analogous estimate based on white rice with mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed tables; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 4. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false because standard Japanese curry roux blocks typically contain dairy (butter/milk solids); is_halal set to false as standard Japanese curry roux may contain pork-derived ingredients; consumers should verify roux ingredients for halal and vegan compliance. 5. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for Japanese vegetable curry rice exists in published ontology releases as of 2026.
